title: using/with keyword asymmetry: two prepositions for one concept
description: Declaring an ambient allocator requirement uses 'using(allocator)'. Binding the allocator uses 'with (allocator: expr)'. These are different prepositions for the same concept. A new reader encounters 'fn foo() void using(allocator)' and 'with (allocator: gpa.allocator())' as separate syntactic constructs and must learn they are two halves of the same feature. Suggested rename: use a single keyword for both. Options: 'ambient(allocator)' / 'provide(allocator: expr)', or 'alloc fn' / 'alloc(expr) { }'. The name matters because it establishes the mental model: is this a capability, a parameter, a scope? The current pair of keywords does not answer that question.
